title: Media, Crime, and Justice
catalogDescription: Investigates interrelationships between crime, justice, and media. Explores representations of crime, systems-impacted individuals, victims, and criminal legal systems as portrayed by various forms of media. Considers the impacts of these portrayals on individuals and the larger culture. Examines how media inform and filter understandings of crime and justice.

title: War on Drugs: Crime and Justice
catalogDescription: Investigates how policymaking, policing, legal, and carceral agencies articulate and execute the U.S. War on Drugs. Critically analyzes prohibition and enforcement, and examines the relationship between policy, mass incarceration, and inequality. Explores changing perspectives on and approaches to prohibition.

effectiveYearRanges.note: Parsed from the official UW Tacoma course descriptions page.

sourceLinks.label: UW Tacoma course descriptions (TCRIM)
sourceLinks.note: Course detail parsed from the official UW course descriptions page.

notes: UW Tacoma catalog metadata parsed from the official UW course descriptions.

title: Miscarriages of Justice
catalogDescription: Explores various types of miscarriages of justice in U.S. criminal legal systems, with significant focus on case studies of the wrongfully convicted and exonerated. Examines research conducted on the causes of wrongful convictions including eyewitness misidentification, flawed forensics, forced confessions, prosecutorial misconduct, ineffective assistance of counsel, and others.

sourceLinks.note: Course detail parsed from the official UW course descriptions page.

title: U.S. Hate Crime and Organized Hate
catalogDescription: Explores definitions and causes of bias-motivated crime in the U.S. and the individual, community, and social consequences of hate crime. Critically analyzes ideologies, recruitment, subcultures, tactics, and activities of organized hate movements, and examines formal legal and collective social efforts to confront hate crime and organized hate groups.

title: United States Federal Law Enforcement
catalogDescription: Examines the structures, jurisdictions, and functions of federal law enforcement. Explores and assesses ethical and legal imperatives to balance civil liberty protections. Analyzes mandates to address national security and criminal activity within the United States and globally. Critically analyzes the actions of federal law enforcement through a social justice lens.

title: Diversity and Social Justice in Criminology
catalogDescription: Explores definitions and implications of diversity and social justice theory. Applies principles of social justice to criminal justice contexts within the United States. Analyzes social positons in relation to relative privilege and power. Emphasizes experiential and reflective learning.

effectiveYearRanges.note: Parsed from the official UW Tacoma course descriptions page.

sourceLinks.note: Course detail parsed from the official UW course descriptions page.

title: Introduction to the Sociology of Deviance and Social Control
catalogDescription: Examines social constructions of norms and deviance, processes used to control deviance, the impact of societal responses to deviance, and cross-cultural variations. Analyzes why some behaviors, differences, and people are considered deviant while others are not. Discusses formal and informal responses to deviance including stigmatization, criminalization, and medicalization. Course equivalent to: SOC 271.

title: Restorative Justice
catalogDescription: Explores the philosophical underpinnings of restorative justice (RJ) and its application in the context of harm-doing, both inside and outside of criminal legal systems. Analyzes the effectiveness, risks, and benefits of RJ practice for RJ stakeholders. Explores the potential to facilitate systems change.

title: White Collar Crime
catalogDescription: Examines harms committed by states, agencies, corporations, and those in white-collar occupations. Explores social definitions, causes, and consequences of these offenses. Critically examines the social contexts that promote such violations, and explores social, legal, and community responses to white collar criminality.

title: Mass Shootings in the United States
catalogDescription: Examines the causes and prevention of mass shootings in the United States. Analyzes social, cultural, psychological, and political factors that contribute to this phenomenon, and the impact of these events on individuals, communities, and society. Explores historical context, motivations, current trends, media coverage, legal and policy issues, and potential solutions.

title: Settler Colonial Crimes Throughout U.S. History
catalogDescription: Explores the range of settler colonial crimes committed against Indigenous peoples throughout the U.S. and its territories. Analyzes the doctrines and ideologies enabling crimes. Examines damage through the perspectives of law, human rights, and social harm. Analyzes Indigenous philosophies and practices of resistance.

title: Women, Crime, and Justice
catalogDescription: Explores the history, societal impact, and future of women within U.S. criminal legal systems. Focuses on factors that contribute to the involvement of women in the criminal legal system, including poverty, trauma, substance use, and major mental illness. The course explores promising and proven gender-responsive approaches for working with system-involved women.

title: Juvenile Delinquency and Society
catalogDescription: Critically examines conceptions of youthful delinquency, looking at its causes, as well as the responses of the juvenile legal system. Analyzes historical and contemporary perspectives on delinquent behavior and explores current trends in juvenile legal system policy and practice, including prevention and intervention efforts.

title: Mental Health, Substance Use and the Criminal Justice System
catalogDescription: Examines the intersection of mental health, substance use, and the criminal justice system. Focuses on the interaction of mental health and criminal justice practice. Explores the prevalence of mental health and substance use and best practices for treatment. Offered: jointly with TSOCWF 361.

title: Criminological Theory
catalogDescription: Surveys the major schools of thought related to the causes of crime and criminalization, positions theories in their historical contexts, and discusses each perspective's assumptions about human nature. Outlines current debates and critiques of various schools of thought and explores the policy implications of each theoretical perspective.

title: The Criminalization of Immigration
catalogDescription: Examines the criminalization of immigration in the United States and globally and the ways in which social institutions have implemented immigration policies. Analyzes the unintended consequences of criminalizing policies and practices. Explores psychosocial effects on the lives of diverse immigrants, their families, and ethnic minority communities. Offered: jointly with TSOCWF 363.

title: LGBTQ+ Persons, Crime, and Justice
catalogDescription: Examines experiences of lesbian, gay, bisexual, transgender, queer (LGBTQ+) persons across criminal legal systems, the criminalization of queerness, and the historical impacts of policy and law. Scrutinizes discriminatory systems practices toward LGBTQ+ people with intersecting identities (race, class, gender, ability). Explores contemporary issues through critical theory and reflexivity.

title: Facing Harm: Victim Offender Dialogue
catalogDescription: Examines the relationship between restorative justice theory and practice. Develops skills in facilitating several models of victim offender dialogue (VOD). Articulates the benefits and risks of VOD for justice stakeholders. Critically examines the effectiveness of VOD as a response to crime and violence. Cultivates a practitioner identity.

title: Police and Society
catalogDescription: Examines the role of police within society through a social justice lens, emphasizing history, public perceptions, administration, organizational culture, and ethics. Examines contemporary concerns and controversies within law enforcement, such as use of force and issues of inequality. Surveys emerging practices in policing and examines alternative models for fostering safety.

title: Interpersonal Skills for the Justice Practitioner
catalogDescription: Focuses on skills needed to establish relationships with systems-involved individuals, including victims, and understand their experiences within a wider social context. Develops skills in interpersonal communications, empathic and collaborative relationship-building, cultural humility, and values-based practice. Explores current models of individual and systemic change. Cultivates a holistic practitioner identity. Prerequisite: a minimum grade of 2.0 in T CRIM 225.

title: Punishment, Incarceration, and Society
catalogDescription: Focuses on the history, structure, and role of punishment and incarceration in society. Explores different theoretical justifications for punishment. Examines various practice and policy issues relevant to contemporary punishment and incarceration, including impacts on system-involved individuals, victims, and communities.

title: Criminal Evidence and Investigation
catalogDescription: Examines crime detection, techniques for case management and documentation, the concept of proof, the impact of emergent technology on the investigative process, interacting with victims and witnesses, and interviewing suspects. Emphasizes the issues that arise during the investigation of crimes such as homicides, sex offences, child abuse, and hate crimes.

title: Human Trafficking
catalogDescription: Examines domestic and international human trafficking, recruitment, and control methodologies. Considers labor and sexual exploitation and examines existing laws and services to combat trafficking. Analyzes the role of demand in perpetuating trafficking and the victims' trauma. Applies best practice solutions. Offered: jointly with TSOCWF 374.

title: Men, Masculinities, Crime, and Justice
catalogDescription: Explores issues related to men and masculinities in the criminal legal system. Examines the various conceptions of masculinities that increase the risks of criminality and how men "perform" masculinities within criminal legal systems.

effectiveYearRanges.note: Parsed from the official UW Tacoma course descriptions page.

title: Harm Reduction
catalogDescription: Examines the history and development of harm reduction approaches to drug use, sex work, and other related issues, and how these strategies can reduce harms and further complications for individuals and communities. Identifies controversies, successes, and challenges and analyses examples of innovative harm reduction policies, practices, and services. Offered: jointly with TSOCWF 376.

title: Reentry Success: Exploring Experiences and Programs for Returning People
catalogDescription: Examines the numerous challenges to successful reentry and integration into the community, after serving sentences in carceral facilities. Provides in-depth exploration of the issues impacting the reentry of returning people. Explores possible solutions to create successful reentry processes and outcomes. Offered: jointly with TSOCWF 377.

title: Criminological Research Methods
catalogDescription: Introduces criminological research methods, including the processes of reviewing scholarly research, forming research questions, designing a study, collecting and analyzing data, and sharing findings. Develops skills in the critical consumption of research. Course overlaps with: TSOCWF 390 and SOC WF 390. Prerequisite: a minimum grade of 2.0 in either QMETH 201, STAT 220, STAT 221/CS&SS 221/SOC 221, STAT 311, TSOCWF 351, TMATH 110, or T URB 225.

title: Criminal Courts and Society
catalogDescription: Examines the background, legal principles, and structures of criminal courts in the United States. Explores the roles of court actors, procedures, and practices which uphold or threaten basic rights and liberties. Focuses on contemporary issues, including the development of alternative and specialized courts. Utilizes a critical, social justice lens to examine criminal courts and their impacts on society.

title: Advanced Readings in Criminal Justice
catalogDescription: Student-initiated, individually contracted course of study targeted at developing greater mastery of a specific area within criminal justice under the supervision of a social work faculty member with expertise in a related area of criminal justice. Focuses on individualized student-centered learning with emphasis on achievement of stated student learning objectives.

title: Racialized Disproportionality Across Systems
catalogDescription: Examines disproportionate representation of people of color in the child welfare, economic, education, health, juvenile justice, and mental health systems. Focuses on how each of these systems interacts with criminal legal systems in which racialized disproportionality is a particularly serious problem. Course overlaps with: T EDUC 490. Offered: jointly with TSOCWF 427.

title: Policy and Practice with Sexual Offenders
catalogDescription: Develops understanding of sex crimes and the people who commit them. Addresses the theoretical explanations of, and policies regarding treatment for offenders. Analyzes laws related to sex offenders, their constitutional legitimacy, and the difficulty in balancing offender and community rights. Offered: jointly with TSOCWF 428.

title: Children of Incarcerated Parents
catalogDescription: Examines the impact of parental incarceration on the psychological, social, physical, and biological development of children. Focuses on issues of loss, trauma, attachment, and ways to address such issues. Offered: jointly with TSOCWF 430.

title: Trauma-Informed Interventions with Systems-Impacted People
catalogDescription: Provides an overview of trauma theory and pathways to trauma transformation. Explores the role of trauma in harm-doing, victimization, and the practices of criminal legal systems. Teaches effective interventions and skills for working with and supporting healing among those who cause harm and those who experience harm as victims. Offered: jointly with TSOCWF 433.

title: Criminal Homicide
catalogDescription: Examines the causes, forms, and consequences of homicide offending and victimization. Explores the patterns and characteristics of various forms of homicide. Analyzes the effectiveness of solutions and interventions both within and beyond criminal legal systems. Course overlaps with: SOC 275.

title: Terrorism, Crime, and Justice
catalogDescription: Explores terrorism and counterterrorism organizations from a criminological perspective. Traces the evolution of domestic and international terrorism organizations and networks within the United States and globally, including motivations and tactics, and the counterterrorism strategies used by law enforcement. Analyzes the social construction of terrorism in academic and popular discourses.

title: Contemporary Social Work in Criminal Justice Settings
catalogDescription: Focuses on social work practice with justice-involved individuals in criminal justice settings including juvenile justice, specialty courts, community corrections, jail and prison. Describes social work roles at multiple levels and examines contemporary issues that impact social work practice. Explores promising social work interventions and what is needed for effective practice. Offered: jointly with TSOCWF 436.

title: Abolitionism and Revolutionary Criminology
catalogDescription: Examines meanings of abolitionism and debates over dismantling the prison industrial complex. Compares criminal justice reforms to more revolutionary transformation of systems. Explores the liberating potential of abolitionist solutions to problems of crime, oppression, and injustice. Imagines transformative alternatives to build humane systems that address harms in the collective interest.

title: Fundamentals of Criminal Law
catalogDescription: Examines the historical, constitutional, and legal principles applicable to substantive criminal law. Analyzes the definition of criminal law, elements of major crimes, general principles of criminal responsibility, punishment, and the conditions that may excuse an individual from criminal liability or mitigate punishment.

title: Senior Seminar: Professionalism and Ethical Issues in Criminal Justice
catalogDescription: Examines the interaction between ethics and criminal justice practice, including application of ethical theory to criminal justice issues. Topics include ethical response to police brutality and corruption in criminal justice systems, development of professional identity, and promotion of professional conduct. Prerequisite: a minimum grade of 2.0 in T CRIM 371; and a minimum grade of 2.0 in TSOCWF 390 or T CRIM 390.

title: Comparative Criminal Justice Systems
catalogDescription: Examines the design, function, and legal basis for non-United States criminal justice systems. Engages cross-cultural analyses of the connection between government, political, demographic, and economic factors in explaining historical and contemporary trends. Compares and contrasts non-United States and United States criminal justice systems. Includes a study abroad component.

title: Independent Research in Criminal Justice
catalogDescription: Student-initiated, individually contracted research with a faculty member to engage the design and implementation of original empirical research. Training and supervision in some or all aspects of criminological/criminal justice research. Active participation as member of research team, with emphasis on achievement of state student learning objectives.

title: Criminology and Justice Studies Internship
catalogDescription: Engages in experiential learning by applying academic knowledge to justice-related professions. Develops professional competencies through working empathetically and effectively with crime- and systems-impacted individuals, families, and communities and/or engaging in policy, administrative, and advocacy work common to justice-related agencies. Prerequisite: a minimum grade of 2.0 in either T CRIM 361/TSOCWF 361, T CRIM 365, or T CRIM 371. Credit/no-credit only.
